segments.title: Live question system setup
segments.summary: The stream explains how audience questions should be submitted so they can be tracked rather than lost in chat.
segments.body: The live-stream setup explains how audience questions should be submitted so they can be answered in sequence rather than disappearing in chat. It also shows why a live Q&A works best when the prompt and the relevant video moment stay connected.

evidence.note: The transcript discusses adding questions to the question system.

segments.title: Modern Navy equivalent of the Blackburn Blackburn
segments.question: What is the modern Navy equivalent of the Blackburn Blackburn for this channel?
segments.answerShort: The prompt is preserved with its video moment so the Blackburn Blackburn comparison can be found from the topic and video pages.
segments.summary: A live Q&A prompt asks for a modern Navy analogue to the Blackburn Blackburn and is preserved as a source-linked segment.
segments.body: The question ties an unusual naval-aircraft comparison to the relevant part of the live Q&A. It is useful for viewers following odd aircraft analogies because the prompt is kept with the surrounding discussion rather than separated from its context.

evidence.note: The transcript contains the modern Navy equivalent question and setup.

segments.title: Spanish 1950s destroyer escorts
segments.summary: A discussion of Spanish Navy destroyer escorts frames them as useful ships, but as gun-and-torpedo designs in a naval world moving beyond that model.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke treats the Spanish destroyer escorts as useful ships whose gun-and-torpedo design sat uneasily in a navy moving toward missiles and broader escort roles. The note separates the ships' practical value from the limits of their Cold War design context.

evidence.note: The transcript discusses Spanish Navy destroyer escorts and gun-and-torpedo destroyer design.

segments.title: Carrier group force structure sketch
segments.summary: A force-structure answer sketches a carrier group built around carriers, LHDs, air-defense destroyers, ASW frigates, and submarines.
segments.body: The carrier-group sketch brings several force elements into one answer: carriers, amphibious aviation, air-defense destroyers, ASW frigates, and submarines. The same video moment is worth following through multiple subjects because Dr. Clarke is describing a layered naval force, not a single ship type.

evidence.note: The transcript lists carriers, LHDs, air-defense destroyers, ASW frigates, and SSNs around a carrier group.
